title: Ensure that the --max-wals argument is set to 0

input_row.title: Ensure that the --max-wals argument is set to 0
input_row.rationale: etcd is a highly-available key value store used by Kubernetes deployments for persistent storage of all of its REST API objects. You should avoid automatic log rotation and instead safeguard the logs in a centralized repository or through a separate log management system.
input_row.audit: Run the following command on the etcd server node: ps -ef | grep etcd Verify that --max-wals argument exists and it is set to 0.
input_row.remediation: Edit the etcd environment file (for example, /etc/etcd/etcd.conf) on the etcd server node and set the ETCD_MAX_WALS parameter to 0: ETCD_MAX_WALS="0" Edit the etcd startup file (for example, /etc/systemd/system/multi- user.target.wants/etcd.service) and configure the startup parameter for --max-wals and set it to \"${ETCD_MAX_WALS}\": ExecStart=/bin/bash -c "GOMAXPROCS=$(nproc) /usr/bin/etcd -- name=\"${ETCD_NAME}\" --data-dir=\"${ETCD_DATA_DIR}\" --listen-client- urls=\"${ETCD_LISTEN_CLIENT_URLS}\" --max-walsr=\"${ETCD_MAX_WALS}\"" Based on your system, reload the daemon and restart the etcd service. For example, systemctl daemon-reload systemctl restart etcd.service Impact: You will have to manage log rotation and archiving.  127 | P a g e Default Value: By default, --max-wals argument is set to 5. References: 1. https://coreos.com/etcd/docs/latest/op-guide/configuration.html#max-wals 2. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/etcd/
input_row.impact: You will have to manage log rotation and archiving.  127 | P a g e Default Value: By default, --max-wals argument is set to 5.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**APPROACH DECISION:**
- The control requires verification of the etcd process startup arguments, specifically the presence and value of the --max-wals flag.
- etcd is often run as a systemd service or as a static pod, but the Kubernetes API does NOT expose etcd process arguments directly.
- The audit procedure specifies running ps -ef | grep etcd on the etcd server node, which requires host-level access.
- There is no standard Kubernetes API or kubectl command to retrieve etcd process flags unless etcd is deployed as a pod and the command/args are visible in the PodSpec (which is not always the case, especially for external etcd clusters).
- Therefore, this check requires manual/host-level inspection.

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. SSH into each etcd server node in your Kubernetes cluster.
2. Run the following command to list the running etcd process and its arguments:
   ```
   ps -ef | grep etcd
   ```
3. Examine the command line for the etcd process and verify that the --max-wals argument is present and set to 0.
   - Example: `... --max-wals=0 ...`
4. If etcd is managed via a systemd service, also review the etcd service unit file (commonly at /etc/systemd/system/etcd.service) and any referenced environment files (such as /etc/etcd/etcd.conf) to confirm that ETCD_MAX_WALS is set to "0".
5. Confirm compliance if all etcd instances have --max-wals=0 set. If any instance is missing the flag or has a different value, the control is not met.

**SOURCE:** CIS KUBERNETES 1.6 BENCHMARK V1.0.0 ARCHIVE
